---
title: Lecture 20
subtitle: Spyder IDE; Scraping Web Tables; Web-scrapping with `selenium`.
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    incremental: true   
    # css: clean-revealjs.css
    # logo: logo-title-slide.png
author:
  - name: Byeong-Hak Choe
    email: bchoe@geneseo.edu
    affiliations: SUNY Geneseo
date: 2024-04-16
execute: 
  eval: false
  echo: true
callout-icon: false

from: markdown+emoji

# bibliography: refs.bib
include-after-body: target-hover.html
---


```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| eval: true

library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
# set default options
opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
               fig.width = 7.252,
               fig.height = 4,
               comment = "#",
               dpi = 300)

knitr::knit_engines$set("markdown")
```


## Announcement

<p align="center">
  <img src="https://bcdanl.github.io/lec_figs/dist-danl210-01-spring-2024-midterm.png" width="850px">
</p>


## Announcement
### Homework Assignments

- There will be two more homework assignments.

- The single lowest homework score will be dropped when calculating the total homework score. 

- Each homework except for the homework with the lowest score accounts for **25%** of the total homework score.




## Announcement
### Project

- The project will be done individually. Each student must work alone.

- Details of the project will be announced next week.
  - The project is about (1) collecting data via web scraping via Python `selenium` and APIs and (2) presenting your findings through descriptive statistics, data visualizations, and `pandas` data analysis on your personal website.

- Think of this project as a more comprehensive homework assignment.

- Your website and its corresponding GitHub repository will be assessed for the project.


## Announcement
### Schedule

- Homework 4 Due: April 30, 2024, Tuesday
- Homework 5 Due: May 9, 2024, Thursday
- Final Exam: May 10, 2024, Friday, 3:30 P.M.-5:30 P.M.
- Project Due: May 16, 2024, Thursday, 11:59 P.M.


# Spyder IDE {background-color="#1c4982"}



## Spyder Environment
### Script Editor

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}

```{r, echo=FALSE, eval = T,  eval = T,out.width = '100%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://bcdanl.github.io/lec_figs/spyder_env.png")
```
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

- From **Script Editor**, we can create, open and edit files.

:::

::::



## Spyder Environment
### Console Pane


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r, echo=FALSE, eval = T, out.width = '100%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://bcdanl.github.io/lec_figs/spyder_env.png")
```
:::


::: {.column width="5%"}

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
- From **Console Pane**, we can interact directly with the Python interpreter, and type commands where Python will immediately execute them.

:::

::::


## Spyder Environment
### Variable Explorer


:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r, echo=FALSE,  eval = T,out.width = '100%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://bcdanl.github.io/lec_figs/spyder_env.png")
```
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
- From **Variable Explorer**, we can see the values of variables, data frames, and other objects that are currently stored in memory.


:::


::::



## Spyder Environment
### Variable Explorer


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="65%"}
```{r, echo=FALSE,  eval = T,out.width = '100%', fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("https://bcdanl.github.io/lec_figs/spyder-var-explore.gif")
```
:::

::: {.column width="5%"}

:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
- If we doucle-click the objects such as `list` and `DataFrame` objects, we can see what data are contained in such objects.

:::

::::


## Coding Workflow on Spyder IDE
### Code and comment style
- The `#` mark is Spyder's comment character.

- Consider using block commenting for separating code sections.
  - `# %%` defines a coding block in Spyder.
  
- To set your keyboard shortcuts,
  - Preferences > Keyboard Shortcuts > Search "run" and/or "comment"
  - Set the shortcuts for (1) run selection; (2) run cell; (3) toggle comment; (4) blockcomment; and (5) unblockcomment


# Scrapping Tables with `pd.read_html()` {background-color="#1c4982"}


## Web-scrapping
### Scrapping Tables with `pd.read_html()`


- Let's scrap the two tables in the following webpage:
  - [National Park Visitation Sets New Record as Economic Engines](https://www.nps.gov/orgs/1207/national-park-visitation-sets-new-record-as-economic-engines.htm)



## Web-scrapping
### Scrapping Tables with `pd.read_html()`

```{.python}
import pandas as pd
url = "https://www.nps.gov/orgs/1207/national-park-visitation-sets-new-record-as-economic-engines.htm"

tables = pd.read_html(url)
len(tables)
tables[0]
tables[1]
```

- `read_html()` read HTML tables into a list of `DataFrame` objects.



## Web-scrapping
### Exporting a `DataFrame` as a **CSV** File with `to_csv()`

:::{.nonincremental}
- To export `DataFrame` as a **CSV** file, we use the `to_csv()` method.
  - Before exporting, you can set the working directory to organize and manage the location of the CSV file. 
  - This helps in keeping your data analysis and exports well-organized.

:::

```{.python}
# Import the os module to interact with the operating system
import os  

# Set the working directory path
wd_path = '/Users/bchoe/My Drive/suny-geneseo/spring2024/lecture-codes/'
os.chdir(wd_path)  # Change the current working directory to wd_path
os.getcwd()  # Retrieve and return the current working directory

# index=False to not write the row index in the CSV output
tables[0].to_csv('table.csv', index =False)
```



## Scrapping Tables with `pd.read_html()`

Let's do [Classwork 11](https://bcdanl.github.io/210/danl-cw/danl-210-cw-11.html)!
